questions.question: Match each PFC subdivision to its notable role:
categories: Rule-selective neurons; oddball/inhibition; shifting
Stop-signal task (right hemisphere); deduction
Social rules, reversal learning, affective input
Relating rules, higher-order goals, exploration

correct_mapping.Orbitofrontal cortex (OFC / vmPFC): Social rules, reversal learning, affective input

questions.explanation: Each PFC subregion has distinctive roles: DLPFC for rule-selective neurons and shifting, VLPFC for stop-signal inhibition, OFC/vmPFC for social rules and reversal, and FPC for relating rules and exploration.

questions.question: Match each basal-ganglia loop to its striatal entry and function:
categories: Putamen — motor control
Anterior caudate — cognitive executive control
Ventral striatum — reward and emotional regulation

correct_mapping.Affective loop: Ventral striatum — reward and emotional regulation

questions.explanation: The three parallel basal-ganglia loops share the architecture Cortex → Striatum → Pallidum → Thalamus → Cortex, but enter different striatal subregions: putamen (motor), anterior caudate (prefrontal/cognitive), ventral striatum (affective).

questions.question: Match each inhibition task to its key characteristic:
categories: Reactive inhibition to a rare stimulus; generates the P300 (M5 practical)
Pre-emptive inhibition; impaired in schizophrenia
Inhibit a response already initiated; sensitive to ADHD and Parkinson's

correct_mapping.Oddball: Reactive inhibition to a rare stimulus; generates the P300 (M5 practical)
correct_mapping.Stop-signal: Inhibit a response already initiated; sensitive to ADHD and Parkinson's

questions.explanation: Oddball tasks (reactive, P300-generating) are the M5 practical. Go/No-Go probes pre-emptive inhibition and is sensitive to schizophrenia. Stop-signal is the most demanding because the response is already underway, making it sensitive to ADHD and Parkinson's.

questions.question: Match each frontal damage syndrome to its hallmark profile:
categories: Lateral PFC damage — apathy, inability to plan or initiate, impaired response selection
Ventral and medial PFC damage — euphoric/manic, unproductive movement, failure to read social cues (e.g. Phineas Gage)

correct_mapping.Frontal dysexecutive syndrome: Lateral PFC damage — apathy, inability to plan or initiate, impaired response selection
correct_mapping.Frontal disinhibition syndrome: Ventral and medial PFC damage — euphoric/manic, unproductive movement, failure to read social cues (e.g. Phineas Gage)

questions.explanation: Dysexecutive (lateral PFC): apathy, planning failures, lab-test impairments. Disinhibition (ventromedial PFC): euphoria, social-cue failures, often normal in lab but chaotic in real life — Phineas Gage is the classic case.

questions.question: Match each form of inhibition to a representative example:
items: Removing irrelevant info from working memory
categories: Suppressing a previously rewarded response
Filtering out distracting flankers during a target task
Withholding profanity in a formal setting
Discarding the now-outdated phone number you just rehearsed

correct_mapping.Preventing irrelevant interference: Filtering out distracting flankers during a target task
correct_mapping.Restraining socially inappropriate behav: Withholding profanity in a formal setting
correct_mapping.Removing irrelevant info from working me: Discarding the now-outdated phone number you just rehearsed

questions.explanation: The book's four forms of inhibition each have everyday analogues: stopping over-trained responses, filtering distractors, suppressing socially inappropriate impulses, and clearing outdated content from working memory.

questions.question: Match each rostro-caudal theory of PFC organisation to its champion and core claim:
categories: Etienne Koechlin — posterior = current-time rules, middle = changing-context, anterior = long-term goals
Badre & D'Esposito — anterior implements higher-order policies that determine which posterior rule applies

correct_mapping.Temporal abstraction: Etienne Koechlin — posterior = current-time rules, middle = changing-context, anterior = long-term goals
correct_mapping.Policy abstraction (hierarchy): Badre & D'Esposito — anterior implements higher-order policies that determine which posterior rule applies

questions.explanation: Both theories agree the PFC is organised rostro-caudally by abstraction. Koechlin frames the gradient temporally; Badre & D'Esposito frame it as anterior policies shaping posterior rules. The professor flagged that neither has 'won' yet.

questions.question: Match each Baddeley working memory component to its content domain:
categories: Auditory / language information
Visual & spatial information
Multimodal information pulled from episodic memory
Control system that allocates resources among the buffers

correct_mapping.Episodic buffer: Multimodal information pulled from episodic memory
correct_mapping.Central executive: Control system that allocates resources among the buffers

questions.explanation: Baddeley's model has three capacity-limited buffers (phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer) and a central executive that allocates processing resources among them depending on task demands.

questions.question: Match each task to the executive process it primarily probes:
categories: Shifting among rules / perseveration
Relating rules — frontopolar cortex
Inhibiting a response already initiated
Conflict monitoring (ACC) on incongruent trials
questions.explanation: Each task isolates a different executive process: WCST tests shifting (perseveration), Progressive Matrices test rule-relating (FPC), stop-signal tests reactive inhibition, and Stroop probes ACC-mediated conflict monitoring.

questions.question: Match each ERP/region to the function it most clearly indexes:
categories: Detection of a rare/oddball target requiring inhibition
Internal awareness that a motor response was wrong
External signal that an action did not produce the desired outcome
Implementation of cognitive control following ACC conflict signal

correct_mapping.P300: Detection of a rare/oddball target requiring inhibition
correct_mapping.Response ERN: Internal awareness that a motor response was wrong
correct_mapping.Feedback ERN: External signal that an action did not produce the desired outcome
correct_mapping.DLPFC activation on incongruent Stroop: Implementation of cognitive control following ACC conflict signal

questions.explanation: The P300 indexes oddball detection. Response ERN follows internally-detected motor errors; feedback ERN follows external error feedback. ACC signals conflict and trial-N+1 DLPFC activity implements the resulting control adjustment.

questions.question: Match each working memory model feature to Baddeley vs Cowan:
items: Capacity limit lives in the attentional spotlight
Central executive allocates resources to buffers
categories: Baddeley
Cowan
Cowan
Baddeley
questions.explanation: Baddeley posits modular buffers and a resource-allocating central executive. Cowan rejects discrete buffers in favour of unitary activated LTM with capacity limited by the attentional spotlight. Both agree on a central executive — the disagreement is about storage architecture.
